coredata.dc:title: Detection of pathogens using graphene quantum dots and gold nanoclusters on paper-based analytical devices
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Hao
Lin, Jia-Hui
Dong, Zhi-Shun
Chen, Wei-Ting
Chan, Yau Kei
Yeh, Yi-Chun
Chang, Huan-Tsung
Chen, Chien-Fu
coredata.dc:description: Pathogens are a persistent threat to human health, causing infectious disease and millions of deaths annually. The accurate and timely detection of pathogens is crucial in disease prevention, treatment, and monitoring. Although reliable detection methods are well established, many of them are still limited in use to clinical laboratories due to the need for costly and specialized instrumentation. In this study, we demonstrate a handheld and low-cost pathogen sensor consisting of a paper-based analytical device (μPAD) that can perform immunoassays and quantify analyte concentration by integrating with an automated color detection system that analyzes the color intensity of the μPAD. The core of the proposed sensor is the portable color detection system that can read the red-green-blue color of the paper emitted light from fluorescent nanomaterials, including graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), which are conjugated with antibodies to indicate the immunoassay results, converting the presence of a pathogen to a colorful fluorescence signal. By adopting GQDs and AuNCs with high quantum yield and relatively high fluorescence intensity as the sensing signal, the paper-based detection system decreases the detection limit to as low as subnanogram/mL. Furthermore, GQDs and AuNCs can emit distinguishable fluorescence under the same light source (UV light) and possess limited background interference from the cellulose, enabling two or more analytes to be simultaneously detected with one UV light. Furthermore, a reaction time of just 10 min is needed, enabling diagnoses to be made in a timely manner and with high sensitivity. As a result, the proposed handheld pathogen sensor can rapidly detect the presence of pathogens with enhanced sensitivity and multiplexity, along with low instrumentation requirements, making it suitable for use in resource-limited settings where medical infrastructure is lacking.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pathogen detection
Paper-based analytical device
Immunoassay
Nanomaterials
On-site detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and surface modification of the μPAD
The synthesis of GQDs conjugated with protein A antibody and AuNCs conjugated with exotoxin A antibody
Protein A and exotoxin A detection procedures
The design of the portable color detection system
Synthesizing nanomaterials for highly sensitive and multiplex pathogen detection
The principle and operation process of the portable pathogen sensor
Optimization of the capture and detection antibody concentrations
Evaluation of the long-term storage stability of the proposed μPAD
Quantification of the protein A and exotoxin A concentrations using the portable color detection system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the nanomaterial and nanomaterial-conjugated antibodies. (A) TEM images of the synthesized (A1) GQDs conjugated with protein A antibody and (A2) AuNCs conjugated with exotoxin A antibody. Scale bars are 100 nm. (B) The hydrodynamic radius of (B1) GQDs with and without the protein A antibody, and (B2) AuNCs with and without exotoxin A antibody. (C) The absorbance spectra of the synthesized (C1) GQDs with and without the protein A antibody, and (C2) AuNCs with and without exotoxin A antibody. (D) Photoluminescence spectra of the GQDs and AuNCs excited at 365 and 470 nm, and their corresponding photographic images under UV illumination. (E) Photoluminescence spectra of paper immobilized without and with GQDs or AuNCs, and the excitation wavelength was 365 nm.

The components and the working principle of the proposed portable pathogen sensor, which consists of a μPAD and color detection system. (A) Schematic of the components of the color detection system. (B) Schematic of protein A and exotoxin A detection on the μPAD. The μPAD is modified with protein A or exotoxin A capture antibodies. When the sample contains protein A or exotoxin A, the μPAD will emit blue or red fluorescence after adding the GQD-conjugated protein A detection antibody or AuNC-conjugated exotoxin A detection antibody, respectively. (C) When the sample contains S. aureus, the antibody-antigen complex will form, resulting in a bright blue fluorescence emitted from the μPAD, and a high green channel value that is displayed on the screen of the color detection system. (D) When the sample contains no S. aureus, no complex will form, resulting in a dim blue fluorescence of the μPAD and a low green value. (E) When the sample contains P. aeruginosa, the exotoxin A capture antibody-antigen complex will form, resulting in a bright red fluorescence on the μPAD and a high red channel value is displayed on the screen. (F) When the sample contains no P. aeruginosa, no complex will form, resulting in a dim blue fluorescence of the μPAD and a low red channel value.

(A, B) Images of the protein A detection results on the μPAD illuminated by UV light and the resulting green channel values for capture and detection antibody concentrations ranging from 100 to 600 μg/mL and 10–60 μg/mL, respectively. The test and control groups for the tests featured protein A concentrations of 100 and 0 ng/mL, respectively. (C, D) Images of the exotoxin A detection results on the μPAD and the resulting red channel values for capture and detection antibody concentrations varying from 100 to 500 μg/mL and 10–30 μg/mL, respectively. The test and control groups for the tests featured exotoxin A concentrations of 100 and 0 ng/mL, respectively.

(A) Images of the protein A detection results after storing the μPAD for 1–6 weeks and the corresponding green channel values. The test and control groups contain 100 and 0 ng/mL of protein A, respectively. (B) Images of the exotoxin A detection results on the μPAD stored for 1–6 weeks and the corresponding red channel values. The test and control groups contain 100 and 0 ng/mL of exotoxin A, respectively.

(A) The schematic of the protein A and exotoxin A detection process. (B) Images of the μPAD immunoassay results with protein A concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 ng/mL, and the corresponding normalized green channel values. (C) Images of the μPAD immunoassay results with exotoxin A concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 ng/mL, and the corresponding normalized red channel values.

The schematic of the proposed pathogen sensor consisted of a color detection system and a μPAD, which integrates with nanomaterials, decreasing the detection limit and enabling multi-target detections.

head.ce:title: Detection of pathogens using graphene quantum dots and gold nanoclusters on paper-based analytical devices

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Applied Mechanics, National Taiwan University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Hao Yuan and Jia-Hui Lin contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pathogens are a persistent threat to human health, causing infectious disease and millions of deaths annually. The accurate and timely detection of pathogens is crucial in disease prevention, treatment, and monitoring. Although reliable detection methods are well established, many of them are still limited in use to clinical laboratories due to the need for costly and specialized instrumentation. In this study, we demonstrate a handheld and low-cost pathogen sensor consisting of a paper-based analytical device (μPAD) that can perform immunoassays and quantify analyte concentration by integrating with an automated color detection system that analyzes the color intensity of the μPAD. The core of the proposed sensor is the portable color detection system that can read the red-green-blue color of the paper emitted light from fluorescent nanomaterials, including graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), which are conjugated with antibodies to indicate the immunoassay results, converting the presence of a pathogen to a colorful fluorescence signal. By adopting GQDs and AuNCs with high quantum yield and relatively high fluorescence intensity as the sensing signal, the paper-based detection system decreases the detection limit to as low as subnanogram/mL. Furthermore, GQDs and AuNCs can emit distinguishable fluorescence under the same light source (UV light) and possess limited background interference from the cellulose, enabling two or more analytes to be simultaneously detected with one UV light. Furthermore, a reaction time of just 10 min is needed, enabling diagnoses to be made in a timely manner and with high sensitivity. As a result, the proposed handheld pathogen sensor can rapidly detect the presence of pathogens with enhanced sensitivity and multiplexity, along with low instrumentation requirements, making it suitable for use in resource-limited settings where medical infrastructure is lacking.

ce:section.ce:para: Pathogens significantly threaten human health by causing various infectious ailments, including respiratory and diarrheal diseases [1–4]. According to a report by the World Health Organization (WHO), such illnesses are a leading cause of death worldwide, particularly in low income countries [5]. To decrease these mortality rates, it is crucial to receive a timely diagnosis by correctly identifying the pathogen strain to guide appropriate treatments [6–8].

Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) are a relatively rapid and simple method of quantitatively detecting pathogens based on the specific binding between antigens and antibodies [9]. These tests are also relatively simpler and more cost-effective compared to other analytical techniques, with comparable or better sensitivity and specificity, in addition to other advantages such as long reagent shelf-lives [10]. However, ELISA have limitations. The results are typically measured using an absorption spectrometer, which requires analysis in a well-equipped lab, hampering the use of ELISA for rapid and on-site pathogen detection. Additionally, ELISA generally consumes large volumes of the sample/reagents, increasing the cost of testing.

To minimize these disadvantages, immunoassays have been integrated with paper-based analytical devices (μPADs), which are particularity suitable for performing these tests as they can reduce the sample/reagent consumption due to the micron/millimeter-size of the fluidic channels on the μPAD [11–15]. As a result, μPAD-based immunoassays have been widely applied to detect the presence of various pathogens including tuberculosis, norovirus, and Escherichia coli [16–18]. In these μPAD tests, a colorimetric substrate (e.g., tetramethylbenzidine (TMB)) or indicator (e.g., gold nanoparticles) are generally used to indicate the detection result [19,20]. For instance, by performing immunoassays with a horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-tagged antibody and TMB, the immunoassay result can be achieved by observing the resulting blue color on the μPAD caused by the reaction between TMB and HRP [21,22]. Gold nanoparticles are another typical colorimetric indicator that are used to tag the antibody. In the presence of the antigen, the tagged antibody will bind with the antigen, causing the gold nanoparticles to aggregate and generate surface plasmon resonance coupling that changes the color of the μPAD [23]. However, using TMB as an indicator requires additional operation step and time to perform the reaction between HRP and TMB, and also requires low temperature storage to maintain the activity of HRP. Additionally, colorimetric immunoassays utilizing TMB or gold nanoparticles as indicators generally feature poor sensitivity [24].

To enhance the simplicity and sensitivity of μPAD-based immunoassays in pathogen detection, we utilize fluorescent nanomaterials to indicate the detection results, specifically graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), which emit blue and red fluorescence, respectively. Typically, the GQDs are doped with nitrogen, resulting in a higher fluorescence intensity and a narrow emission band. The AuNCs are synthesized with bovine serum albumin (BSA), in which process the high reduction capability of the Tyr residues on BSA helps to produce highly fluorescent AuNCs. Benefit from the high fluorescence intensity of the GQDs and AuNCs, the detection limit can be decreased to as low as 0.1 ng/mL. In addition, the GQDs and AuNCs can emit distinguishable fluorescence under the same light source, enabling multiplexed immunoassay tests to be performed with the proposed μPAD. As proof-of-concept, Staphylococcus aureus and Pseudomonas aeruginosa were used as target species, which we simultaneously detected by tagging the corresponding antibodies with GQDs and AuNCs, respectively, with the resulting μPAD tests emitting bright blue or red fluorescence under a UV light. Additionally, we show the fluorescence can be analyzed by an automated handheld detection system, allowing users to directly read the results. The entire process takes only 10 min and features. Compared to conventional colorimetric μPAD-based immunoassays, this new fluorescent design requires less reaction time and demonstrates enhanced multiplicity. In addition, the fluorescent signals can be immediately captured and analysed by an Arduino-integrated homemade color detection system, enabling the operator to directly achieve the detection results of the proposed nanomaterials-based immunoassay. By integrating color sensor into the detection system, the results of the of μPAD can be read the red-green-blue color from different photoluminescent nanomaterials without the need for additional optical equipment. As a result, the proposed design enables portable and rapid detection of multitarget pathogen, which can hardly be achieved by previously reported μPAD-based immunoassays. Furthermore, this color detection system is with a significant low cost of under $50, and independent of commercial software licenses. Therefore, we believe that this proposed pathogen sensor integrating fluorescent nanomaterials with a μPAD device and portable detection system paves the way for simple and multiplexed on-site pathogen detection with higher sensitivity, increasing the outreach of diagnostics and healthcare to low-resource settings.

Exotoxin A secreted by P. aeruginosa and protein A located on the surface of S. aureus were used as target antigens to detect P. aeruginosa and S. aureus, respectively. Specifically, recombinant P. aeruginosa exotoxin A regulatory protein (toxR), capture toxR antibody, and detection toxR antibody were purchased from Cusabio (Cusabio Biotech, Wuhan, China). Protein A, biotin-protein A antibody, capture anti-protein A antibody, and protein A detection antibody were purchased from GeneTex (GeneTex Inc., Irvine, CA, USA). The streptavidin conjugation kit was purchased from Abcam (Abcam, Cambridge, MA). Streptavidin, (+)-biotin N-hydroxysuccinimide ester (biotin NHS ester), ethylenediamine (EDA), tetrachloroauric acid, bovine serum albumin (BSA), CaCl2, sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), N-ethyl-N′-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), sodium acetate buffer solution (NaOAc buffer, pH 5.2), phosphate buffered saline (PBS), and PBS-Tween 20 (PBS-T) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Sigma–Aldrich, St. Louis, USA). Sodium citrate dihydrate and sodium hydroxide were purchased from J.T. Baker (Phillipsburg, NJ, USA). Deionized water was obtained from a Milli-Q system (Millipore, Milford, MA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and surface modification of the μPAD
ce:section.ce:para: The device was fabricated from Whatman cellulose chromatography paper (grade 1 Chr sheets) based on our previously developed method [12]. In brief, the cellulose paper was first patterned with detection wells using a wax printer, and then heated at 120 °C for 10 min to facilitate the penetration of the wax in between the cellulose fibers, resulting in hydrophilic detection wells of 4-mm diameter circle surrounded by hydrophobic wax on the cellulose paper.

To modify the fabricated detection wells with capture antibody, we first treated the well with 100 μL of 25 mM CaCl2 containing 5 mg/mL CMC for 10 min, and then with 100 μL of 10 mM NaOAc buffer containing EDC (19 mg/mL) and NHS (46 mg/mL) for 30 min. The resulting detection well was then dried at 30 °C for 10 min, followed by the addition of 3 μL of the capture antibody (used to bind and retain the analytes) for 1 min at 30 °C.

GQDs were synthesized by first mixing EDA containing 1 g/mL citric acid with 15 mL deionized water. The resulting solution was then stored in an autoclave at 200 °C for 4 h. Afterward, the solution was dialyzed in deionized water for 10 h using a 3.5 K molecular-weight cut-off dialysis membrane. The resulting solution was stored at 4 °C [25].

The synthesis of AuNCs was conducted by adopting a method proposed by Xie et al. [26]. Typically, 0.1 mL of NaOH (1 M) was added to a stirring solution containing 1 mL tetrachloroauric acid (10 mM) and 1 mL BSA (50 mg/mL). Afterward, the solution was stored at 37 °C for 16 h, followed by dialysis in deionized water for 10 h with a 3.5 K molecular-weight cut-off dialysis membrane. The dialyzed solution was then stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis of GQDs conjugated with protein A antibody and AuNCs conjugated with exotoxin A antibody
ce:section.ce:para: The conjugation between the GQDs and protein A detection antibody (used to bind with the capture antibody-absorbed protein A to form the antigen-antibody complex) was conducted by mixing the biotin-tagged GQDs and streptavidin-tagged protein A detection antibody. Specifically, the biotin-tagged GQDs were achieved by diluting the GQD solution (described in the previous section) 10-fold using deionized water and mixing it with 22 mg/mL biotin NHS ester. The excess unreacted biotin molecules were removed using a 1 K dialysis membrane. After dialysis, the resultant biotin-tagged GQDs was stored at room temperature for 4 h. To tag the protein A detection antibody with streptavidin, we mixed 160 μg/mL biotin protein A detection antibody with 156 μg/mL streptavidin at a volume ratio of 1:1, which helped to prevent the streptavidin from becoming saturated with the protein A detection antibody. Afterward, the GQD-conjugated protein A detection antibody can be achieved by mixing the biotin-tagged GQDs with the streptavidin-tagged protein A detection antibody at a 1:1 volume ratio. The resulting solution contained 40 μg/mL of protein A detection antibody.

The synthesis of the AuNC-conjugated exotoxin A detection antibody was completed by mixing the biotin-tagged AuNCs with streptavidin-tagged exotoxin A detection antibody, similarly to the synthesis of the GQD-conjugated protein A detection antibody. To tag AuNCs with biotin, the AuNC solution (described in the previous section) was diluted 1.2-fold using deionized water and then mixed with 22 mg/mL biotin NHS ester at a 10:1 volume ratio. The solution was then stored at room temperature for 4 h. The binding between streptavidin and exotoxin A detection antibody was completed with a streptavidin conjugation kit. Specifically, we first dissolved 100 μg of streptavidin in an aqueous solution containing 10 μL of the modifier reagent from the kit and 100 μL of 1 mg/mL exotoxin A detection antibody. The resulting solution was mixed with the quencher reagent from the kit. The achieved streptavidin-tagged exotoxin A detection antibody was diluted to 50 μg/mL and then mixed with the biotin-labeled AuNCs at a volume ratio of 1:1 for 30 min to obtain the AuNC-conjugated exotoxin A detection antibody.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protein A and exotoxin A detection procedures
ce:section.ce:para: To detect protein A, 3 μL of 250 μg/mL protein A capture antibody was first added onto the surface of the modified detection wells of the μPAD and rested at 30 °C for 2 min, followed by the addition of 3 μL of 1 wt% BSA (blocking buffer) at 30 °C for 2 min, then washing with PBS-Tween 20. Afterward, 100, 30, 10, 3, 1, 0.3, and 0.1 ng/mL of protein A (antigen) were added onto separate detection wells which were then treated with the GQD-conjugated protein A detection antibody (containing 40 μg/mL protein A detection antibody) and heated at 30 °C for 2 min. After washing the detection well with PBS-Tween 20, the detection results can be achieved by analyzing the green channel value of the well using the portable color detection system (see below for details). Exotoxin A detection was completed following the same procedures only using exotoxin A capture antibody and the AuNC-conjugated exotoxin A detection antibody solutions instead.

To optimize the reaction conditions, including the capture antibody concentration, the μPAD-based immunoassay results were imaged using a smartphone (ZenFone, ASUS, Taiwan) and the resulting images were analyzed by ImageJ to determine the red and green channel values (National Institute of Health, USA). After optimizing the reaction conditions, the quantification of the analyte concentrations was completed using the proposed color detection system to analyze the red and green channel values of the μPAD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The design of the portable color detection system
ce:section.ce:para: The portable color detection system was used to measure and display the color intensity of the μPAD and consisted of (1) a protective shell, printed by a 3D printer, (2) an Adafruit TCS34725 sensor (Taiwan Intelligent Sensor Technology, Tainan, Taiwan) for sensing the RGB values of the μPAD, (3) an Arduino UNO device (Arduino.cc, Ivrea, Italy), which is a low-cost, open-source USB data acquisition device, for analyzing the color intensity, and (4) a screen (Taiwaniot, Tainan, Taiwan) to display the red and green channel values (Fig. S1).

To characterize the nanomaterials, solutions containing the synthesized GQDs and AuNCs were first drop-cast on carbon-coated copper grid substrates (200 mesh, Jia Yu Apparatus Co. Ltd, Taiwan), which were then kept in vacuum at room temperature and examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H-7100, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 100 kV. The fluorescence spectra of the nanomaterials were measured using a spectrophotometer (F-7000 FL Spectrophotometer, Hitachi, Japan). The hydrodynamic radius and the absorbance of the nanomaterials was characterized using dynamic light scattering (Malvern Nano-ZS90, UK) and UV–visible spectroscopy (Jasco V-730, US) respectively. A wax printer (Xerox 8570, Fuji, Japan) was used to pattern the cellulose paper. The double-distilled water was generated from a Sartorius arium® mini plus Lab Water System (Göttingen, Germany).

In this study, fluorescent nanomaterials including AuNCs and GQDs were integrated with the μPAD-based immunoassay to perform the immunoassay for pathogen detection. Instead of the enzyme or gold nanoparticles used in the traditional immunoassays, using fluorescent nanomaterials to conjugate with antibodies for indicating immunoassay results can significantly enhance the multiplicity of the μPAD-based immunoassay owing to the distinctive emission band of the nanomaterials. In addition, the results of μPAD-based immunoassay can be directly analyzed and displayed on a home-made color detection system, which integrates an Arduino and is with a data acquisition rates up to 860 Hz. Smartphone can also be integrated into the proposed μPAD device; however, there can be variation in camera image sensor among different brands and models of smartphone, which would further complicate the integration. By integrating color sensor into the detection system, the results of the of μPAD can be read the red-green-blue color from different photoluminescent nanomaterials without the need for additional optical equipment. As a result, the pathogen concentration can be directly achieved using the proposed nanomaterial-based μPAD immunoassay and the color detection system, independent of any external instrument as shown in Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesizing nanomaterials for highly sensitive and multiplex pathogen detection
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials are promising biotags for biological images and diagnostics due to their high quantum yield, photostability, and tunable optical properties [27–31]. Among various fluorescent nanoprobes, GQDs and AuNCs have been widely developed for fluorescent on/off detection and biomedical applications [32–35]. Compared with organic dyes, both have simple synthetic routes, excellent water solubility, and high biocompatibility [36,37].

GQDs possess one or a few layers of graphene and connected chemical groups on the edges [38]. Compared to the mental-based QDs, the GQDs are composed of the non-toxic carbon, and can emit strong fluorescence under UV light, making GQDs a particularly useful tool in bioimaging and diagnosis [38,39]. Typically, in diagnosis, GQDs with a high fluorescence intensity are preferred since it can potentially help to lower the detection limit of a diagnostic test. To enhance its fluorescence intensity, we doped the GQDs with nitrogen by applying EDA as the dopant. The doped nitrogen acts as the illuminate centers, bringing a new energy level to GQDs, improving the utilization of photon energy and resulting in a high quantum yield, and ultimately enable GQDs to emit strong fluorescence due to the n-π * transition [40,41].

AuNCs, referring to ultrasmall gold particles with a diameter smaller than 3 nm, are becoming one of the promising fluorescent nanomaterials for biosensing and bioimaging [42,43]. To synthesize AuNCs, reducing agents such as sodium borohydride, sodium citrate and ascorbic acid are generally used for the reduction of Au3+ ions, producing highly fluorescent, water-soluble AuNCs. Nevertheless, using chemical reduction generally requires a long reaction time and produces undesired byproducts [26]. Therefore, instead of these conventionally used chemical reduction, we use BSA which is the most abundant plasma protein, as both reducing agent and a stabilizer to synthesize AuNCs. The Tyr residue in the BSA possesses reduction capability, converting Au ions to Au0 through their phenolic groups and significantly reducing the production of the undesired byproduct [26]. In addition, the synthesized AuNCs were coated with BSA molecules, facilitating the further surface modification of AuNCs with functional ligands.

To integrate these fluorescent indicators with the proposed μPAD-based immunoassays, we respectively tagged GQDs and AuNCs to the protein A and exotoxin A detection antibodies using the binding between the streptavidin and biotin interaction. After binding with the detection antibodies, the GQDs and AuNCs can still maintain their dispersibility as shown in Fig. 1A, helping to ensure the accuracy of the immunoassay. Additionally, the hydrodynamic radius of the GQDs changes from 10.8 nm to 26.7 nm, and the absorbance of the GQDs shift from 349 nm to 352 nm, indicating a successful binding between GQDs and protein A detection antibody as shown in Fig. 1B1 and C1; for the AuNCs, the binding between AuNCs and exotoxin A detection antibody can only be verified by the change of hydrodynamic radius (from 6.9 nm to 20.8 nm), since the absorbance shift is undetectable due to the strong absorbance of the high concentration of BSA used in the synthesis of AuNCs, as shown in Fig. 1B2 and C2. Furthermore, the GQDs and AuNCs conjugated antibodies can be used for multiplex immunoassays due to their distinguishable emission bands (GQDs (~440 nm) and AuNCs (~670 nm)) (Fig. 1D), enabling different immunoassay results to be visualized simultaneously under UV light. Notably, the two paper substrates immobilized with GQDs and AuNCs show non-overlapping emission bands excited at 365 nm, which should allow them to be distinguished from each other by the color detection system (Fig. 1E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The principle and operation process of the portable pathogen sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Our proposed portable pathogen sensor is made up of a μPAD to perform the immunoassay for the detection of pathogens and a color detection system to analyze the result on the μPAD ( Fig. 2A, B, and Video S1). Specifically, the proposed μPAD consists of a hydrophilic detection well surrounded by hydrophobic wax (Fig. 2B). The portable color detection system consists of a UV light for illuminating the μPADs, a color sensor, and a microcontroller board for sensing and analyzing the color intensity of the μPADs, as shown in Fig. 2A. To demonstrate the feasibility of the sensor, we selected S. aureus and P. aeruginosa bacteria as the target pathogens since they are the most common causes of chronic wound infection and are frequently found together [42]. Because S. aureus and P. aeruginosa are Biosafety Level 2 (BSL-2) pathogens, clinical samples containing S. aureus and P. aeruginosa should be handled in a BSL-2 laboratory. To detect the bacteria, we use protein A, which is a highly stable cell surface receptor of S. aureus [44], and exotoxin A, which is secreted by P. aeruginosa [45], as the target antigens to indicate the presence of these two pathogens.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1.
Video S1
.

To detect protein A, a sample solution is first added onto the detection well of the μPAD, which was previously modified with protein A capture antibody, followed by the addition of blocking and washing buffers and the GQD-conjugated protein A detection antibody in sequence (see Experimental Section for more details). When the sample contains protein A, it is captured by the antibody on the modified well and forms the antigen-antibody complex, resulting in a bright blue fluorescence (~450 nm) under UV light (365 nm). The blue fluorescence is quantified by inserting the μPAD into the color detection system, leading to a high green value displayed on the system as shown in Fig. 2C. However, when the sample contains no protein A, the antigen-antibody complex cannot be formed, resulting in a relatively dim fluorescence emitted from the residual GQD-conjugated antibodies that are physically adsorbed on the μPAD. The color analyzer therefore measures a low green value in the detection well, as shown in Fig. 2D.

The same operation procedures can be applied for the detection of exotoxin A. When the sample contains exotoxin A, the detection well emits the bright red fluorescence (~670 nm) of the AuNC-tagged antibody-antigen complex, resulting in a high red value, as shown in Fig. 2E. Similarly, the detection well emits dim fluorescence and results in a low red value when the sample contains no exotoxin A, as shown in Fig. 2F. Note, the images in Fig. 2E and F appear purple due to the influence of the blue fluorescence emitted from the μPAD itself under UV illumination.

As a result, by conjugating the antibodies with fluorescent nanomaterials, immunoassay test results can be achieved directly without the need for performing enzymatic reactions, which are more common in conventional colorimetric-based immunoassays and require refrigeration during transportation and storage to maintain the activity of the enzyme. In addition, the detection of protein A and exotoxin A can be completed with the proposed sensor, requiring no external instrument and enabling the on-site detection of pathogens. Furthermore, compared to the traditional ELISA, the proposed paper-based device has the advantages of reduced consumption of sample and antibody volume, shortened assay time, and inexpensive reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the capture and detection antibody concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: The capture and detection antibody concentrations are crucial for the proposed immunoassays on the μPAD since excess antibodies will lead to a high background noise and insufficient antibodies will compromise the sensitivity. To avoid these undesired conditions, we optimized the capture and detection antibody concentrations in the proposed protein A and exotoxin A detection tests. We first performed protein A detection on the μPAD using different protein A capture antibody concentrations ranging from 100 to 600 μg/mL while keeping the antigen (100 ng/mL in the test group and 0 ng/mL in the control group) and GQD-conjugated-protein A detection antibody (20 μg/mL) concentrations constant. Then we imaged the μPADs and analyzed their green channel values using ImageJ, as shown in Fig. 3A. The maximum green channel value difference between the test and control group indicates the optimal reaction condition, which was achieved when the protein A capture antibody concentration was 250 μg/mL. We then optimized the GQD-conjugated-protein A detection antibody concentration by varying it from 10 to 60 μg/mL and keeping the antigen (100 ng/mL in the test group and 0 ng/mL in the control group) and capture antibody concentration constant. A maximum green channel value between the test and control group was achieved when the GQD-conjugated-protein A detection antibody concentration was 60 μg/mL, as shown in Fig. 3B. However, the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity increases from 0.89 (detection antibody concentration 40 μg/mL) to 3.45 and 9.28 when the detection antibody concentration reaches 50 μg/mL and higher. Therefore, we chose 40 μg/mL as the optimal detection antibody concentration due to its relatively small standard deviation and high green channel value difference, as shown in Fig. 3B. Consequently, an optimal protein A detection condition can be achieved when the capture antibody is 250 μg/mL and the detection antibody is 40 μg/mL.

Similarly, to determine the optimal reaction condition for exotoxin A detection, we varied the exotoxin A capture antibody concentration from 100 μg/mL to 500 μg/mL and adjusted the detection antibody concentration from 10 μg/mL to 30 μg/mL. The results showed that a maximum red channel value difference is achieved, indicating the optimal reaction condition, when the capture antibody concentration is 250 μg/mL and the detection antibody concentration is 25 μg/mL, as shown in Fig. 3C and D.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the long-term storage stability of the proposed μPAD
ce:section.ce:para: In practical scenarios, the stability of the μPAD during storage and transportation is of great importance. To examine the stability, we stored the μPADs for 1–6 weeks at 4 °C in a refrigerator and performed the protein A and exotoxin A detection tests. The green and red channel values of the μPAD tests (using the optimized conditions) remained relatively constant as we increased the storage time, as shown in Fig. 4A and B, verifying the μPAD can be used to perform pathogen detection up to 6 weeks of storage. The stability of the μPAD may be due to the disruption of the interaction of the cellulose paper hydroxyl groups with the antibodies used in the immunoassays. Specifically, the hydroxyl groups promote denaturation of the antibody [46]. In the proposed μPAD, the undesired hydroxyl groups are eliminated by grafting them with CMC and EDC/NHS groups in the μPAD surface modification step (see Experimental Section for more details). Therefore, with the proposed μPAD, the activity of the antibodies can be preserved, ensuring the stability of the device even after long-term storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of the protein A and exotoxin A concentrations using the portable color detection system
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the prior experiments, we determined the optimal conditions for detecting protein A and exotoxin A on the μPAD and established standard curves for these target analytes using the proposed color detection system. Specifically, we performed separate immunoassays on each μPAD using 0.1–100 ng/mL of protein A or exotoxin A based on the established detection process, and then inserted the resulting μPADs into the portable color detection system for RGB analysis, recording the green or red values of each μPAD displayed on the screen. We normalized these values (Gnormalized or Rnormalized) using the following equations:
(1) Gnormalized = (G1-G0)/G0
Rnormalized = (R1-R0)/R0 (2)

in which G1 is the measured green value and G0 is the green value when the protein A concentration is 0 ng/mL; and R1 is the measured red value and R0 is the red value when the exotoxin A concentration is 0 ng/mL. We found that Gnormalized increases with the protein A concentration, and Rnormalized increases as we increase the exotoxin A concentration, as shown in Fig. 5. The linear ranges of protein A and exotoxin A in the semi-log plot were 0.3–30 ng/mL with detection limits of 0.2 and 0.1 ng/mL (at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3), respectively. These results show the protein A and exotoxin A detection results on the μPAD can be achieved directly from the color detection system without the need for additional steps, such as image taking or ImageJ analysis, enabling on-site detection of the pathogens (Table S1). Additionally, the proposed handheld sensor can read the green and red channel values of the μPAD under UV light without the use of additional light filters. Consequently, protein A and exotoxin A can be simultaneously and quantitatively detected with the proposed rapid, easy-to-use, and portable pathogen sensor.

In this study, we develop a portable pathogen sensor that consists of a μPAD that is used to perform immunoassay-based pathogen detection and a color analysis system for quantifying the detection results. For the μPAD, fluorescent GQD and AuNC nanomaterials are conjugated with the antibodies and indicate the detection result. To lower the detection limit of the nanomaterial-based immunoassay, the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs and AuNCs are enhanced by doping the GQDs with nitrogen and synthesizing AuNCs with BSA, resulting in a detection limit as low as subnanogram/mL. Owing to the distinguishable fluorescence colors and narrow emission band of the GQDs and AuNCs, multiple antigens can be analyzed simultaneously, enhancing the multiplexity of the μPAD-based immunoassay. To validate the feasibility of the proposed pathogen sensor, we demonstrated protein A and exotoxin A detection, which are used to indicate the presence of S. aureus and P. aeruginosa. Due to the separate nanomaterial-tagged antibodies (using GQDs and AuNCs, respectively), the protein A and exotoxin A concentrations can be detected simultaneously in just 10 min. Additionally, by inserting the resulting μPAD into the proposed Arduino-integrated color detection system, unknown protein A and exotoxin A concentrations can be quantified by simply reading the green/red values displayed on the analyzer according to the standard curve of the displayed values versus the antigen concentrations. As a result, the proposed pathogen detection system can be used to simultaneously measure and analyze two or more pathogens simply by using different nanomaterials, such as nanodiamonds or carbon dots, as fluorescent indicators, increasing the multiplicity of this μPAD-based immunoassay design. By placing the appropriate optical filters in front of the color sensor, the proposed detector enables multiplexed detection of more than two target pathogens. Additionally, the system features enhanced portability compared to current μPAD-based pathogen detectors, which require more complex instrumentation to conduct electrochemical- and nucleic-acid-based sensing [47,48]. Unlike previous fluorescent sensors, the proposed sensing system enables portable, rapid, multiplexed pathogen detection in resource-limited areas, and it can provide an accurate and timely diagnosis of bacterial infection to prevent opportunistic infections and antibiotic resistance.
H.Y., J.H.L., and C.F.C. designed the research; H.Y., J.H.L., Z.S.D., and W.T.C. performed the experiments; all authors participated in analyzing the data and writing the paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan (109-2223-E-002-004-MY3, 110-2923-E-002-004-MY3), and the Higher Education Sprout Program at National Taiwan University (111L890904).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data and video associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131824.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hao Yuan is now an Assistant Professor in the School of Life Science and Engineering at Southwest Jiaotong University, China. She obtained her Ph.D. degree in Mechanical Engineering, The University of Hong Kong with Prof. Ho Cheung Shum in 2019. Afterward, she joins Professor. Chien-Fu Chen’s group as a postdoctoral researcher in the Institute of Applied Mechanics, National Taiwan University. Her current research focuses on digital nucleic acid amplification, microfluidics-templated materials, and engineered interfaces.

Jia-Hui Lin is currently an Assistant Professor in the Department of Applied Chemistry at the National University of Kaohsiung. She received her Ph.D. from the Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-sen University. Afterward, she was a postdoctoral researcher of Prof. Chen’s research group in the Institute of Applied Mechanics, National Taiwan University. Her research interests include nanomaterial synthesis, heavy metal ion sensing, and chemosensors.

Zhi-Shun Dong is now an engineer of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co., Ltd. He obtained his M.S. degree from the Institute of Applied Mechanics, National Taiwan University. His research interests include biosensors, semiconductor manufacturing, and optic system development.

Wei-Ting Chen is currently a Ph.D. student of Prof. Chen’s research group in the Institute of Applied Mechanics, National Taiwan University. He obtained his M.S. degree from the Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry at the National Chung Cheng University. His research interests include surface chemistry, nanomaterial synthesis, and biosensors.

Yau Kei Chan received his Ph.D. in 2014 from the Department of Ophthalmology, LKS Faculty of Medicine, the University of Hong Kong and is currently the Assistant Professor in the same department. His current research interests lie in the design and testing of ophthalmic biomaterials, surgical tools and diagnostic tools, and the development of eye-on-a-chip microfluidic platforms for vision science research.

Yi-Chun Yeh received her Ph.D. in the Chemistry Department of Stanford University, USA, in Dec of 2010. Following graduation, she worked as a postdoc fellow at the Lawrence Berkeley National Lab from Jan 2011 to July 2012. She is now holding a Professorship in the Chemistry Department of National Taiwan Normal University (NTNU), Taiwan, since Aug. of 2019. Her research interests include protein engineering, whole-cell-based biosensor, and nanomaterial-based sensors.

Huan-Tsung Chang is currently a Distinguished Professor in the Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University. In 1994, he received his Ph.D. from the Department of Chemistry, Iowa State University. He has been obtained several honors, including Fellow of the Royal Society of Chemistry (2013), Outstanding Research Award from National Science Council of Taiwan (2007), Academic Achievement Award from the Chinese Chemical Society (2015), and Highly Cited Researchers from Clarivate Analytics (2017 and 2018). His current research interests include nanotechnology, green chemistry, and biosensors.

Chien-Fu Chen is an Associate Professor at the Institute of Applied Mechanics at National Taiwan University, Taiwan. He obtained his Ph.D. at the Institute of Applied Mechanics from National Taiwan University in 2007. He received the Outstanding Research Award (2022), Futuretech Award (2020), Futuretech Breakthrough Award (2019), and Youth Scholar Grant Award (2018) from the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan. Prof. Chen’s research focuses on miniaturized systems utilizing micro/nanomaterials for biomedical point-of-care diagnostic applications.
